title: Chapter 6: What the Other Knows
content: The hymnals ran out after two weeks.
I'd been stealing them from the chapel — the thin, velvet-covered books with their gilded pages and their songs nobody at Blackwood actually sang. The backs were blank. Perfect for diagrams.
"Show me again," Heath said.

I uncapped the stolen marker — taken from the supply closet when Professor Renfrew was lecturing on civic duty and not watching his things — and drew a rough oval on the back of Blessed Assurance. The oval was a dinner party. I marked positions like a general marking battalions.

"Whoever sits at the head is obvious. That's declared power. Boring." I drew an X at the top of the oval. "It's the person three seats to the left who matters. The one close enough to hear everything, far enough to be forgotten."
Heath leaned over my shoulder. I could smell the cold on his jacket — he'd come from outside, from the boathouse, from something he still didn't talk about. His breath was warm against my ear.
"And the one who leaves early?" he asked.

"Either the most powerful person in the room or the least. You have to watch their hands when they stand. If their hands are steady, they're bored because they've already won. If their hands are busy — fixing a cuff, smoothing a skirt — they're running."
He was quiet. Then: "You see all of this? At every dinner?"

"Every dinner. Every reception. Every funding gala where my mother paraded me like a prize mare." I drew another X. "Sloane Ashworth's father sat two seats from the Chancellor last October. But his hands wouldn't stop moving. Adjusting his tie. Drumming his ring on the glass. I looked it up later — the audit started in September."
"You looked it up."
"I always look it up."
Heath took the marker from my hand. His fingers brushed mine — rough calluses, split knuckle — and he drew a line through the center of my oval. "What's this?"
"A divide. Old money on one side. New money on the other."
"And the scholarship kids?"
I looked at him. "There are no scholarship kids at those tables."
"Exactly." He set the marker down. "People like me are the furniture. We clear the plates. We pour the wine. And nobody watches what the furniture sees."

The conservatory hummed around us — the low drone of heat through old pipes, the green smells of soil and rot and things still stubbornly alive. A dead vine tapped the glass like a visitor who'd forgotten why it came.
"Every dinner party is a battlefield," I said. "You just don't know the weapons yet."
He looked at my diagram — the Xs and lines and arrows mapping the invisible choreography of power. Then he looked at me.
"Teach me," he said.

The boathouse smelled like wet wood and motor oil. The lake threw shattered moonlight through the gaps in the shutters, and the cold came up through the floorboards like something rising from the deep.
Heath held the pads — cracked foam wrapped in duct tape, borrowed from the equipment shed. He held them the way someone holds something they've held a thousand times before.
"Hit them," he said.
I hit them. It was pathetic. My wrist buckled on impact, my shoulder absorbed the force wrong, and the pain shot down my arm like electricity through a bad wire.
"Again."
I hit them again. Worse this time. My knuckles scraped the edge of the pad and split.
"You're thinking," he said. "Stop thinking."
"You told me—"

"I told you to read rooms. This isn't a room. This is your body. It doesn't need analysis." He lowered the pads and walked closer. Close enough that I could see the scar tissue on his knuckles even in the dark. "What do you feel when Sloane Ashworth corners you in the hallway? When she says those things about your family?"
"Rage."
"And what do you do with it?"
"Nothing. I swallow it."
"Swallowed rage is poison. It makes you small and sick and slow." He raised the pads again. "Rage is not the enemy of control. It's fuel. You just have to learn how to burn it."
I hit the pads. Harder. My form was still wrong — my elbow flared, my hip didn't rotate — but there was something behind the strike now. Heat instead of mechanics.
"Better," he said. "Again."

I hit until my knuckles bled properly, until the blood smeared the duct tape red-black, until my shoulders burned and my breath came in ragged pieces. I was bad at it. Then I was less bad. Then I was — not good, but effective. There was a difference.
I grinned. Couldn't help it. My mouth tasted like iron and sweat and something fierce that I'd never let myself taste before.
Heath lowered the pads. He was smiling too — barely, a crack in the stone.
"You've been wanting to do that your whole life," he said.
I didn't deny it.
"Try again," I said. "And this time, don't just tell me what you see. Tell me what it means."

We were back in the conservatory. Dead leaves had accumulated in the corners, and the air had the sour-sweet smell of decomposition that the heating system couldn't quite chase away. I'd spread four more hymnals across the potting bench, each one covered in my handwriting — power maps, hierarchy trees, lines of leverage drawn in stolen ink.
Heath picked up the one labeled ASHWORTH and studied it. His eyes moved the way mine did when I read a room — scanning, cataloguing, calculating.
"Sloane Ashworth isn't cruel," he said slowly, testing the words. "She's terrified. Her father is being audited and she's performing power before she loses it."
"Good."
"Whitfield — the one who always defends her — he's not loyal. He's bargaining. Sloane's family owes his family money, and he's collecting through social credit."
"Better."
He looked up. "And Sloane knows it. That's why she lets him orbit. She thinks proximity is the same as control."
"That's my read."
"Then she's wrong." He set the hymnal down. "Proximity is vulnerability. The closer someone stands, the easier they can cut you."
I stared at him. He'd held that hymnal for maybe ninety seconds, and he'd pulled three deductions I hadn't written down. Not because I hadn't thought of them — because I hadn't thought they mattered.
"Where did you learn that?" I asked.
"Foster care." He said it without emotion, the way you'd name a city you passed through once. "You learn to read people fast when they're deciding whether to keep you."
He turned one of the hymnals toward me. My diagram of the faculty hierarchy — deans, department heads, the Chancellor at the top like a cherry on a poisoned sundae.

"You see them as a structure," he said. "I see them as a story. Renfrew's afraid of Varma because she published first. Varma's afraid of the Chancellor because he controls the endowment. And the Chancellor—" He tapped the top of the page. "He's afraid of exposure. Which means he's the most dangerous and the most vulnerable. Same position."
"You should be seen as a poor boy," I said. "Let them. Poor boys are invisible."
"Invisible boys hear everything."
"Invisible boys know where the bodies are buried."
"Invisible boys don't exist in the record." He looked at me. "Which means they can't be blamed for what disappears."

We looked at each other. And then — I don't know which of us started it — we laughed. Real laughter, ugly and surprised, the kind that comes out before you can stop it. The conservatory glass rattled with it. The dead vines shivered.
It was the closest to happy either of us had been at Blackwood.

The chapel at midnight was a different building. No votives, no organ drone, no performative piety. Just stone and silence and the lingering ghost of incense — resinous, churchy, clinging to the cold air. Our footsteps echoed and died.
"This is the salad fork," I said, holding up three fingers. "This is the fish fork. This is the dessert fork. You use them in order, outside in."
"Who decided this?"
"Dead Frenchmen. That's not the point. The point is that if you use the wrong fork, people notice. And if people notice you, they watch you. And if they watch you—"
"They see what I am."
"They see what you're not." I folded my arms. "You need to be boring. Boring is safe. Boring is the kid who scholarships through without making a sound."
"Scholarship isn't a verb."
"It is when I use it."

He almost smiled. Then he straightened, and his face went smooth and empty as a wiped screen. He picked up an imaginary fork — perfect grip — and mimed eating with mechanical precision. He looked like every bland, forgettable boy in every dining hall at Blackwood.
"Invisible," he said.

"No. That's not invisible. That's trying not to be seen. There's a difference." I stepped closer and adjusted his posture — shoulders back a fraction, chin up. "Invisible is being seen and immediately forgotten. Like a painting you walk past a hundred times and can never describe. You don't shrink. You diffuse."
I showed him the smile — thin, unconfrontational, warm enough to seem genuine but not enough to invite closeness. The smile my mother taught me before she stopped smiling altogether.
"Now you," I said. "Teach me something."
He considered. Then: "Don't answer."
"Don't answer what?"

"Anything you don't want to. Not with a lie — lies are work. Just don't answer. Let the silence sit. Let them fill it. People will say the most extraordinary things to avoid the discomfort of silence. They'll explain themselves. Contradict themselves. Hand you ammunition and load the gun."

I practiced. He asked me questions — Where are you from? Why did you come to Blackwood? What happened to your family? — and I swallowed my answers. I let the questions hang. The silence in the chapel was enormous. It pressed against the stained glass like something trying to get in. And in that silence, Heath's eyes flickered — a tell, a confession, a desire to fill the space himself.
He didn't.
"Good," he said. "You're learning."
We were becoming fluent in each other's native language.
The snow came in the night, silent as a secret.

By morning, the conservatory was a greenhouse made of white — every pane frosted, the world outside reduced to a smudge of grey. The heating pipe had given up sometime after three. My breath hung in front of me like smoke.
Heath arrived with something under his arm. A metal bucket, dented, stolen from the groundskeeper's shed. Inside: newspaper, dry twigs, a broken chair leg he'd split into kindling with his thumbnail.
"What are you doing?"
"Building a fire."
"Here?"
"Watch."

He crumpled the newspaper. Arranged the twigs in a cone — small at the center, larger at the edges. Lit a match from the book he kept in his sock and touched it to the paper's edge. The flame caught, quick and hungry, then settled as it reached the wood. He fed it slowly, piece by piece, the way you'd feed something that might bite you if you moved too fast.
"Learned that in a group home in Bridgeport," he said. "No heat in January. Six kids, one bucket, one book of matches. You learn or you freeze."

The fire grew. Small and contained, an orange eye blinking in the frozen room. The heat reached me in waves — the front of my legs first, then my hands, then my face. The metal bucket ticked as it expanded. The kindling popped and hissed.

The smell: burning wood, old varnish from the chair leg, the mineral tang of the bucket, and underneath it all, the green rot of the conservatory slowly warming and exhaling its humid breath. Snow tapped the glass — the only sound besides the fire and our breathing.

I was sitting cross-legged on the stone floor. Heath sat beside me, close enough that his shoulder was an inch from mine. Close enough that I could feel the heat coming off him too — a different kind, denser, alive.
The dead vines on the far wall threw shadows in the firelight. They looked like reaching hands, like something drowning that couldn't stop grasping.
I leaned into him.
Accidentally at first — my body shifting toward warmth the way a plant shifts toward light. Then, feeling his shoulder solid against mine, I didn't move away. I let myself stay.

He stiffened. Every muscle in his arm locked. I could feel the calculation running behind his ribs — is this a maneuver, a strategy, a trap? He was cataloguing the threat the way I'd taught him to catalogue a room.
Then, slowly, like ice giving way, he relaxed.
Neither of us spoke. The fire crackled. The snow fell. The shadows reached and reached.
I thought: This is the first time I've been warm at Blackwood.
I didn't think about what that meant. I didn't think at all.
Weeks passed. Or they didn't pass — they compressed, the way time does when you're learning something that rewrites your bones.
We stopped needing words.

In the dining hall, across the room, I watched Sloane Ashworth hold court at her table. Her voice carried — bright, sharp, designed to cut. Her friends laughed at a volume that announced their loyalty. Her hands were busy with her water glass. Constantly adjusting.

I touched the scar on my palm under the table. The one I'd gotten the night of my acceptance — the envelope had a jagged edge, and I'd been too distracted to notice the blood until my mother screamed.
Thirty feet away, Heath sat with the other scholarship students. His posture was perfect — unremarkable. He was eating with the right fork. He was wearing his invisibility like a second skin.
He touched his palm.
Same hand. Same gesture. Same scar — different story, same shape.
We didn't nod. We didn't need to.
Sloane laughed again. Her wine sloshed. The friend beside her — Whitfield — leaned in too close, and Sloane's smile didn't reach her eyes.
Heath's gaze met mine for exactly one second. In that second, an entire conversation: She's losing it. He's pressing. The audit is close. She's going to do something desperate.
And then his eyes said something else. Something that made my pulse tick up like a meter in a falling elevator.
Not if. When.
We'd become a single weapon in two bodies. The question wasn't whether we'd use it.
The question was what we'd cut first.
